preamble: The shift from the alveolar nasal [n] to a rhotic [r] is a subtype of rhotacism — the replacement of a non-rhotic consonant by an r-like sound. While the most famous instance of rhotacism converts [s] or [z] to [r] (as in Latin and Ancient Greek), the nasal-to-rhotic path is also attested and shares a phonetic basis: nasals and rhotics are both alveolar, relatively sonorous, and share perceptual similarity in certain contexts. The shift is best documented in Romanian and its dialects, where intervocalic [n] became [r] in Istro-Romanian and some northern dialects (e.g. Latin *bonum > standard Romanian bun, but Istro-Romanian bur; Latin *bene > Istro-Romanian bire; Latin *fenestra > Romanian fereastră). The change is also cited in some Middle Indo-Aryan transitions and in certain Goidelic Celtic dialects where consonant clusters containing /n/ developed rhotic outcomes.

examples.note: Intervocalic /n/ shifted to /r/ in Istro-Romanian and some northern Daco-Romanian dialects. Standard Romanian retains bun.
The /n/ in the Latin source shifted to /r/ in Romanian, though intermediate steps may have involved dissimilation.
In Scottish Gaelic (and Manx), Proto-Celtic initial /kn-/ developed into /kr-/, with the nasal shifting to a rhotic. The resulting vowel is often nasalized, preserving a trace of the original nasal.
Occasional nasal-to-rhotic shifts documented in Middle Indo-Aryan dialects, particularly in consonant clusters and word-internal positions. Exact forms vary by dialect and period.

sources: Cardona, G. & Jain, D. (eds.) (2003). The Indo-Aryan Languages. Routledge.
